stem: A 30-year-old G2P0 woman with SLE diagnosed seven years ago is reviewed in a combined obstetric medicine clinic at 11 weeks gestation. She has been in clinical remission for two years on hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily and low dose prednisolone 5 mg daily. Her one previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage at 9 weeks with no known cause identified at the time. Her most recent rheumatology bloods three months ago confirmed normal complement, negative dsDNA, and positive anti-Ro at 120 U/mL (positive >10) and positive anti-La at 70 U/mL. She has no past history of lupus nephritis, no thromboembolism and her antiphospholipid antibody screen has been repeatedly negative on three occasions over the last two years. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily and aspirin 100 mg daily for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis at 11 weeks. She has no other medical problems, no allergies and no relevant family history. Her partner is well with no family history of congenital heart disease or arrhythmia. The obstetric medicine team plans regular antenatal review and is finalising the foetal monitoring plan with the paediatric cardiologists. She specifically asks about the relevance of her antibody pattern to the baby's heart and her own ongoing medications.
lead_in: Which surveillance plan is the most appropriate addition for this pregnancy?

options.text: Serial foetal echocardiograms from 16 to 28 weeks for congenital heart block
options.rationale: Anti-Ro and anti-La cross the placenta and can cause neonatal lupus, of which congenital complete heart block is the most serious feature. Australian and EULAR guidance is to perform serial foetal echocardiography (typically weekly to fortnightly) between 16 and 26-28 weeks in anti-Ro positive women (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital Vic; EULAR SLE pregnancy guidelines).

options.text: Stop hydroxychloroquine at the start of the second trimester now
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is continued throughout pregnancy in SLE because it reduces flares, congenital heart block recurrence in subsequent pregnancies and possibly the risk of pre-eclampsia (EULAR; RANZCOG).

options.text: Empirical IVIG infusions from 16 weeks to prevent congenital heart block
options.rationale: IVIG has not been shown to reliably prevent first-occurrence congenital heart block and is not standard of care; surveillance with echo and prompt intervention if first-degree block is detected is preferred (EULAR).

options.text: Stop aspirin because anti-Ro positive SLE increases bleeding risk
options.rationale: Aspirin is recommended for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis in SLE from before 16 weeks; anti-Ro positivity does not change this (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: Routine empirical low molecular weight heparin from 12 weeks gestation
options.rationale: LMWH prophylaxis is indicated for antiphospholipid syndrome or significant VTE risk, neither of which apply here; SLE alone without these features does not mandate LMWH (RCOG; RANZCOG).

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman who emigrated to Australia from a high HBV prevalence country is reviewed at 26 weeks gestation in the obstetric medicine clinic. She was diagnosed with chronic hepatitis B 8 years ago during routine antenatal screening in her first pregnancy. She is HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, anti-HBe negative, and her HBV DNA at 24 weeks is 2.3 x 10^8 IU/mL. Her ALT is 28 U/L, platelets 220 x10^9/L, INR 1.0 and her FibroScan was 5.6 kPa six months ago, consistent with minimal hepatic fibrosis. She has no co-infection with HIV, HCV or HDV on booking serology and no clinical features of chronic liver disease. She has no other medical problems, is on no regular medication and has no known drug allergies. Her first child, now four years old, was born in her country of origin and received hepatitis B immunoglobulin and vaccine within 12 hours of birth and is hepatitis B negative on subsequent serology with documented anti-HBs response. She asks specifically what the team will do to reduce the risk of her current baby getting hepatitis B given how high her viral level is, and is keen to breastfeed if possible. She has been counselled that vertical transmission is the dominant route of childhood acquisition.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate maternal intervention to reduce vertical transmission in this pregnancy?

options.text: Hepatitis B immunoglobulin to the mother at 28 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Maternal HBIG does not have a role in reducing vertical transmission; HBIG is administered to the infant after birth and to non-immune individuals after high-risk exposure (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Entecavir 0.5 mg daily from 28 weeks until 4-12 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Entecavir is not preferred in pregnancy because of more limited safety data; tenofovir disoproxil is the agent of choice for HBV in pregnancy (ASHM; GESA).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg daily from 28 weeks until 4-12 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Australian and international guidance recommends maternal tenofovir disoproxil from approximately 28 weeks gestation for women with HBV DNA above 200,000 IU/mL (or HBeAg positive with high VL) because this substantially reduces the residual vertical transmission risk that remains despite infant immunoprophylaxis (ASHM; Gastroenterological Society of Australia; AASLD/EASL).

options.text: Routine elective caesarean section at 39 weeks to reduce transmission risk
options.rationale: Mode of delivery does not influence HBV vertical transmission rates when standard immunoprophylaxis is provided to the infant (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Advise no breastfeeding because of high maternal HBV DNA levels here
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is safe in HBV-positive mothers provided the infant has received standard HBIG and the birth dose vaccine; high maternal DNA is not a contraindication (Australian Immunisation Handbook; ASHM).

stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 woman with a bileaflet mechanical mitral valve replacement for rheumatic heart disease attends preconception clinic with her cardiologist and an obstetric physician. Her rheumatic disease was acquired in childhood before she immigrated to Australia at age 12; surveillance now demonstrates only the prosthetic valve as a residual lesion. She has been on warfarin 4 mg daily with stable INR 2.5-3.5 since her surgery five years ago, monitored at a community anticoagulation clinic, with no thrombotic or bleeding events. She is currently in sinus rhythm, NYHA functional class I with no exertional symptoms during stair climbing, and her most recent transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal mechanical valve function with no regurgitation, no left atrial enlargement, no pulmonary hypertension and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%. She has no other medical problems, no history of thromboembolism, no allergies and is on no other regular medication. She has commenced folic acid 5 mg daily and would like to conceive in the next three to six months. She asks the obstetric medicine team how her anticoagulation will be managed in pregnancy and what trade-offs she should understand. She has no contraindication to either warfarin or low molecular weight heparin and her partner is well. The cardiology team will share care with obstetric medicine throughout the pregnancy and a multidisciplinary management plan is being prepared in advance of any conception attempts.
lead_in: Which anticoagulation strategy is most appropriate to plan from conception through to delivery?

options.text: Use therapeutic-dose enoxaparin with anti-Xa monitoring for the entire pregnancy from positive test until 36 weeks then switch to unfractionated heparin
options.rationale: LMWH throughout is associated with a higher rate of valve thrombosis than warfarin-containing strategies, particularly in mechanical mitral valves; ESC reserves this strategy for women who decline warfarin after counselling (ESC 2018).

options.text: Continue warfarin at the same dose from conception through to elective induction at term to minimise mechanical valve thrombosis risk throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Warfarin throughout pregnancy minimises maternal thromboembolism but at the cost of warfarin embryopathy (6-12 weeks) and foetal bleeding at delivery; pure warfarin throughout is rarely chosen without a structured discussion of trade-offs (ESC 2018).

options.text: Switch to therapeutic-dose enoxaparin from positive pregnancy test, return to warfarin from 13-36 weeks, switch back to LMWH from 36 weeks to delivery
options.rationale: ESC and RANZCOG guidance recommend dose-adjusted LMWH (with anti-Xa monitoring) in the first trimester to avoid warfarin embryopathy when the daily warfarin dose is moderate to high, warfarin in the second and early third trimester for stable INR and lowest thrombotic risk on the valve, then LMWH again from 36 weeks to allow safe neuraxial anaesthesia and delivery (ESC 2018 Cardiovascular Disease in Pregnancy; RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch to rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily preconception and continue this direct oral anticoagulant throughout the entire pregnancy and puerperium
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants are contraindicated in pregnancy and ineffective in mechanical valves; they have no role here (ESC 2018; AMH).

options.text: Stop all anticoagulation in the first trimester to avoid warfarin embryopathy and reintroduce therapeutic LMWH at 14 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Mechanical mitral valves are at very high thrombotic risk and never go unanticoagulated; stopping in any trimester is contraindicated (ESC 2018).

stem: A 36-year-old G3P2 woman delivers a healthy 3.6 kg infant by emergency caesarean section at 39+1 weeks for failure to progress in the first stage of labour. Her BMI on booking was 33 kg/m2 and her current weight is 92 kg with proportionate gestational weight gain. She is otherwise well with no personal or family history of thrombosis, no known thrombophilia and no immobilising disease. The caesarean was technically straightforward with estimated blood loss 600 mL, no transfusion required and no postpartum haemorrhage. She is now on the postnatal ward six hours after delivery, observations stable, mobilising with assistance, no signs of bleeding, lochia normal and the wound dressing dry. Her two previous deliveries were both by vaginal birth at term without complications or postpartum thromboembolic events. Her booking bloods were normal including a normal platelet count and renal function, and she has no allergies. She does not smoke and is in stable accommodation with supportive family. The obstetric registrar is asked to write a postnatal VTE prophylaxis plan based on her current risk profile, including her BMI, age, emergency caesarean and parity. She is keen to breastfeed.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate VTE prophylaxis plan for this patient?

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily orally for 7 days post-discharge
options.rationale: DOACs are not recommended for postpartum thromboprophylaxis in Australia, particularly in women who are breastfeeding; LMWH is preferred (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Mechanical thromboprophylaxis with TED stockings alone for the duration of admission
options.rationale: Mechanical prophylaxis alone is insufficient for a patient with multiple risk factors plus emergency caesarean; pharmacological prophylaxis is required (RCOG Green-top 37a).

options.text: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily for 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Therapeutic dose anticoagulation is reserved for treatment of confirmed VTE or for very high-risk prophylaxis (e.g. recent VTE, antiphospholipid syndrome with prior thrombosis); this patient meets prophylactic, not therapeutic, criteria (RCOG Green-top 37a).

options.text: Prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for at least 10 days from 6-12 hours postoperatively
options.rationale: Emergency caesarean plus age >=35 plus BMI >=30 plus parity >=3 each adds points on Australian and RCOG postnatal VTE risk scoring; this combination indicates pharmacological prophylaxis for at least 10 days postpartum. Enoxaparin 40 mg daily is appropriate at her weight (RCOG Green-top 37a; RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg orally daily for 6 weeks postpartum as VTE prophylaxis
options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended as routine postnatal VTE prophylaxis in this risk profile; LMWH provides the most evidence-based reduction in VTE postpartum (RCOG Green-top 37a).

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 36+4 weeks presents to the maternity assessment unit with 36 hours of nausea, vomiting and right upper quadrant pain. She is increasingly fatigued and her partner reports she has been progressively confused for the last few hours, with intermittent disorientation to time and place. There is no headache, no visual disturbance, no vaginal bleeding and she reports no reduced foetal movements but feels generally unwell. She has had a normal antenatal course in this pregnancy with two prior uncomplicated term vaginal births. She is not on any regular medication, has no allergies and has no personal or family history of liver disease. She does not smoke or drink alcohol and there is no recent travel or sick contacts. On arrival her BP is 138/88 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 98% on air and she appears jaundiced with mild asterixis. Her abdomen is tender in the right upper quadrant without rebound; CTG shows a normotypical baseline of 145 bpm with reduced variability. Her bedside capillary glucose is 2.6 mmol/L. Urgent bloods reveal an ALT of 540 U/L, AST 520 U/L, bilirubin 78 micromol/L, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 95 x10^9/L, ammonia 92 micromol/L, urate 0.46 mmol/L and creatinine 138 micromol/L. Lactate is 4.2 mmol/L and the blood film shows no schistocytes.
lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours acute fatty liver of pregnancy over HELLP syndrome?

options.text: Mild hypertension with a urine PCR above 30 mg/mmol
options.rationale: Hypertension and proteinuria are core features of pre-eclampsia/HELLP and are less characteristic of AFLP, where they may be absent or mild (RANZCOG).

options.text: Right upper quadrant pain with raised transaminases above 500 U/L
options.rationale: RUQ pain and transaminitis are shared between HELLP and AFLP; this feature alone does not discriminate (Swansea criteria).

options.text: Platelet count below 100 x10^9/L on this presentation
options.rationale: Thrombocytopenia <100 x10^9/L is part of HELLP criteria and can occur in AFLP from synthetic failure; it does not preferentially identify either (RANZCOG).

options.text: Maternal hypoglycaemia with raised ammonia and coagulopathy out of proportion to platelet drop
options.rationale: Hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia and a coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, raised INR) that is disproportionate to the relatively modest thrombocytopenia are highly suggestive of acute fatty liver of pregnancy, reflecting hepatic synthetic failure rather than microangiopathic haemolysis (Swansea criteria; RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital Vic).

options.text: Serum urate above the upper limit of normal in the third trimester
options.rationale: Hyperuricaemia is associated with pre-eclampsia/HELLP and not specific to AFLP; it does not discriminate (RANZCOG).

stem: A 24-year-old G0 woman presents to ED at 02:00 with sudden-onset severe right iliac fossa pain that woke her from sleep 4 hours ago, associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. The pain is colicky, radiates to the right groin and posterior thigh, and is currently 9/10 in intensity despite paracetamol 1 g PO taken 90 minutes ago. She had a recent pelvic ultrasound 2 weeks ago for cyclical pelvic discomfort showing a 6 cm right ovarian dermoid (mature cystic teratoma); she was awaiting an outpatient gynaecology appointment. Her last menstrual period was 10 days ago, regular 28-day cycles, and she has no intermenstrual bleeding or discharge. She is sexually active in a long-term relationship, uses condoms, and beta-hCG taken on arrival is negative. She has no fevers, no urinary symptoms and no diarrhoea or rectal bleeding. Her partner reports she has not eaten or drunk anything since the pain began. She has no relevant past medical history, no allergies, and takes only ibuprofen as required for cyclical pain. On examination she is in obvious distress, splinted on the right, with cool peripheries from pain. She has not previously had any abdominal or pelvic surgery and has never been pregnant.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for serial observation, IV analgesia and a formal radiology TVUS at 08:00
options.rationale: Delaying definitive assessment risks complete vascular compromise and ovarian loss; torsion is a time-critical diagnosis and surgical exploration should not wait for elective imaging (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: Emergency CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast before any surgical decision
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the imaging of choice; CT adds delay and radiation without changing surgical management when ultrasound already shows an enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow and a known dermoid (RANZCOG).

options.text: Right salpingo-oophorectomy via laparotomy because the ovary is likely non-viable
options.rationale: Even ovaries appearing dusky or black at surgery should be detorted and reassessed because parenchyma often recovers; oophorectomy as initial action sacrifices fertility unnecessarily (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: Urgent diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian-preserving cystectomy under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, an enlarged ovary with peripheral follicles and reduced Doppler flow on TVUS, and a known ovarian mass strongly suggest ovarian torsion; emergency laparoscopy with detorsion and conservative cystectomy preserves ovarian function. Doppler flow is unreliable for excluding torsion and clinical suspicion should drive surgery (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: IV opioid analgesia and discharge with outpatient gynaecology follow-up next week
options.rationale: Discharging a patient with suspected torsion is unsafe; emergency surgical management is the standard of care (RANZCOG).

stem: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman attends a vulval clinic with a 12-month history of progressive vulval pruritus, soreness, and dyspareunia limiting intercourse with her long-term partner. She has noticed white, paper-thin skin around the labia minora and perineum, with occasional fissuring and contact bleeding when itching at night and during attempts at intercourse. Multiple courses of clotrimazole pessaries and over-the-counter hydrocortisone 1% over the past 6 months have not helped. She has had no postmenopausal bleeding from the cervix and her last cervical screening test 12 months ago was HPV not detected. She has Hashimoto's thyroiditis on stable thyroxine 75 mcg daily, well-controlled hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 28, and no immunosuppression. She has not used local or systemic oestrogen replacement. She has no personal or family history of vulval, cervical, breast or other gynaecological cancer. On examination there is well-demarcated 'figure-of-eight' white atrophic change involving the labia minora, clitoral hood and perianal skin, with partial resorption of the labia minora, narrowing of the introitus, and early posterior fourchette adhesion. There is one persistent indurated nodule 8 mm in diameter on the right labium majus that has been present for 6 weeks and has not responded to topical hydrocortisone. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Punch biopsy the indurated nodule and start clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classic for lichen sclerosus; treatment is ultrapotent topical steroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) typically nightly for 4 weeks, alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance. The 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC means any persistent indurated, ulcerated or pigmented lesion must be biopsied promptly (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Trial topical oestrogen cream for presumed atrophic vaginitis and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: The findings (figure-of-eight white atrophic change, architectural loss) are classic for lichen sclerosus, not generalised atrophic vaginitis; topical oestrogen would not address the diagnosis or the suspicious nodule (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly and review again in 12 weeks
options.rationale: Although ultrapotent topical steroids are the mainstay treatment for lichen sclerosus, the new indurated nodule must be biopsied first because lichen sclerosus carries a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma, and an indurated persistent lesion is suspicious (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the entire affected vulval area
options.rationale: Vulvectomy or wide excision is not the standard treatment for lichen sclerosus; medical control with ultrapotent steroids is first-line and surgery is reserved for biopsy-proven malignancy or symptomatic architectural distortion (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Refer to dermatology for systemic methotrexate as steroid-sparing therapy now
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for refractory disease unresponsive to ultrapotent topical steroids; it is not first-line, and the immediate clinical priority is biopsy of the suspicious nodule (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

stem: A 28-year-old G1P1 woman presents to her GP with a 3-day history of progressively painful right-sided labial swelling. The swelling is now egg-sized, tense, exquisitely tender and is interfering with sitting and walking. She has had a low-grade fever of 37.8 degC at home but has not had rigors. She has had no abnormal vaginal discharge for the past 3 months, no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, no urinary symptoms and no constitutional symptoms. She is in a monogamous relationship of 5 years and her most recent STI screen 4 months ago was negative for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis and HIV. She had a similar but smaller episode on the same side 2 years ago that resolved with incision and drainage in ED but recurred 3 months later as a painless cyst that has since fluctuated in size. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, is not pregnant (LMP 10 days ago, regular cycles), and has no other significant medical history. The GP clinic has Word catheters in stock, the GP is trained in their insertion under local anaesthetic, and the patient consents to a procedure today after written information.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of this Bartholin abscess?

options.text: Simple incision and drainage with packing, no catheter or marsupialisation
options.rationale: Simple I&D without a Word catheter or marsupialisation has a high recurrence rate (up to 40%); the duct often re-closes once the cavity collapses, particularly in a patient with prior recurrence (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).

options.text: Outpatient oral antibiotics alone (e.g. amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BD) without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic monotherapy without drainage rarely resolves a tense fluctuant abscess; drainage is the primary intervention, with antibiotics reserved for systemic features, cellulitis, immunosuppression or pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Incision and drainage with Word catheter insertion retained in situ 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: Word catheter placement after incision and drainage is the recommended primary-care option for an acute Bartholin abscess: it maintains an epithelialised tract while the cavity heals, with a recurrence rate substantially lower than simple I&D. The catheter is removed after 4-6 weeks (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).

options.text: Excision of the Bartholin gland under general anaesthetic as definitive management
options.rationale: Excision is reserved for recurrent abscesses unresponsive to drainage with Word catheter or marsupialisation, or for suspected Bartholin gland carcinoma (especially in women over 40 with a solid mass); it carries higher morbidity (bleeding, dyspareunia) than less invasive options (RANZCOG).

options.text: Sitz baths and oral analgesia, with re-review in 1 week if not improving
options.rationale: Conservative measures are insufficient for a tense fluctuant abscess; while sitz baths help drainage of small early lesions, definitive drainage is required for an established abscess of this size (KEMH Bartholin abscess).

stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 woman has been taking tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 4 years as adjuvant endocrine therapy for ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative, node-negative left breast cancer treated 4 years ago with breast-conserving surgery and adjuvant radiotherapy. She was previously postmenopausal (natural menopause age 52, no bleeding for 12 years prior to commencing tamoxifen) and is now referred to gynaecology after presenting to her GP with two episodes of fresh vaginal bleeding over the past 3 weeks, each lasting 2-3 days and requiring two pads daily. She has had no postcoital bleeding, no discharge, no pelvic pain, no abdominal bloating and no urinary or bowel symptoms. Her last cervical screening 18 months ago was HPV not detected on a clinician-collected sample. She has hypertension (perindopril 5 mg daily, BP 138/82 on review), a BMI of 31, and well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.9%, metformin 1 g BD). She has no personal or family history of Lynch-associated cancers and meets no Lynch testing criteria. She has never used hormone therapy or unopposed oestrogen pre-tamoxifen. She takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents and has no history of VTE. She is fully adherent to tamoxifen, has not missed doses, and is keen to complete her planned 10-year adjuvant course but is anxious that bleeding may signal cancer. Her breast oncologist's letter notes a low risk of breast cancer recurrence on validated risk calculators but does not advise on gynaecological management. She has had no abnormal surveillance mammograms.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discontinue tamoxifen and substitute an aromatase inhibitor without further gynaecological investigation
options.rationale: Stopping tamoxifen unilaterally compromises breast cancer outcomes; substitution decisions belong to the breast MDT after gynaecological diagnosis is established. Tamoxifen change does not address the immediate need to exclude endometrial pathology (eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding on tamoxifen requires structural and histological assessment. Tamoxifen causes subepithelial stromal change and cystic atrophy that thicken the endometrium on TVUS even in benign disease, making the standard >4 mm threshold less reliable; hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is the recommended investigation in any symptomatic tamoxifen user. Routine TVUS surveillance of asymptomatic tamoxifen users is NOT recommended in Australian guidance (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Repeat TVUS in 3 months to assess for any change in endometrial thickness
options.rationale: Repeat imaging delays diagnosis without changing the indication for histology in a symptomatic tamoxifen user; TVUS findings in this population are also poorly specific for malignancy due to tamoxifen-related cystic stromal change (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

options.text: Reassure as endometrial thickness is acceptable for tamoxifen users; review if bleeding recurs
options.rationale: The standard >4 mm threshold cannot be applied to symptomatic tamoxifen users, and any postmenopausal bleeding on tamoxifen requires histological exclusion of cancer; reassurance is unsafe (eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Commence routine 6-monthly TVUS surveillance and biopsy only if thickness exceeds 8 mm
options.rationale: Asymptomatic surveillance of tamoxifen users with TVUS is NOT recommended in Australian guidance; surveillance does not improve outcomes and frequently triggers unnecessary investigations. Bleeding, not thickness, is the investigation trigger in tamoxifen users (eviQ Tamoxifen; RANZCOG).

stem: A 3-year-old girl with known HbSS sickle cell disease, on daily penicillin V prophylaxis and folic acid, is brought to a regional ED by her mother. Over the last 24 hours she has become progressively lethargic, with poor oral intake but no vomiting or diarrhoea. Her older brother had a febrile illness with a malar rash one week ago. She has not had any cough, dyspnoea, chest or limb pain, no preceding bony pain and no haematuria. She is afebrile today, but two days ago had a low-grade temperature. Vaccinations are up to date and her last transfusion was 4 months ago. On examination she is pale and tired, alert but quiet, with no scleral icterus, no rash, clear chest and no neurology. Her abdomen is soft with a non-tender spleen palpable 2 cm below the costal margin, unchanged from her baseline. Observations: T 36.7 degC, HR 142, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA, BP 92/56, capillary refill 2 s. Bedside bloods: Hb 48 g/L (baseline 85), WCC 6.8 x10^9/L, platelets 256, reticulocytes 12 x10^9/L (baseline approximately 300), bilirubin 24 micromol/L, LDH mildly elevated. Mother brought her in because of pallor noticed at bath time.
lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?

options.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis
options.rationale: Splenic sequestration is characterised by a sudden enlargement of the spleen by 2 cm or more from baseline with a brisk reticulocyte response and signs of hypovolaemic shock; this child has an unchanged spleen and a suppressed reticulocyte count (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

options.text: Hyperhaemolytic crisis from delayed transfusion reaction
options.rationale: Hyperhaemolysis presents with jaundice, dark urine, raised LDH and bilirubin and a brisk reticulocytosis, typically within 1-3 weeks of transfusion; her last transfusion was 4 months ago and the reticulocyte count is suppressed (BSH guideline).

options.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from poor dietary intake
options.rationale: Iron deficiency develops over weeks to months and would not drop the haemoglobin by nearly half in 24 hours; reticulocytes in chronic iron deficiency are typically normal rather than profoundly suppressed (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

options.text: Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 infection
options.rationale: A rapid fall in haemoglobin with an inappropriately low reticulocyte count (here 12 against a baseline of approximately 300 x10^9/L) in a child with chronic haemolysis is diagnostic of an aplastic crisis. Parvovirus B19, the cause of fifth disease (the brother's malar rash), suppresses erythroid precursors for 7-10 days and is the typical precipitant (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; eviQ).

options.text: Vaso-occlusive bone marrow infarction
options.rationale: Marrow infarction causes severe pain rather than asymptomatic anaemia, and is usually associated with leucocytosis and an appropriate reticulocyte response (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).
